ontology.foodex2_gap_note: No dedicated FoodEx2 code exists for chicken satay with peanut sauce; A0FKP (grilled chicken) used as closest defensible parent anchor with sauce component noted.
ontology.snomed_ct_gap_note: No SNOMED CT food concept found for chicken satay; omitted to preserve ontology honesty.

serving.natural_language_portion: A typical hawker serving of chicken satay consists of 5 skewers (approximately 150 g total including peanut sauce), commonly ordered as a snack or starter at Singaporean hawker centres.

nutrient_source.title: Chicken Satay with Sauce — HPB Singapore Food Composition Database
nutrient_source.source_match_notes: Nutrient values sourced directly from HPB Singapore Foodo database entry for Chicken Satay with Sauce (F11020021). All core macros and secondary nutrients are taken from this canonical entry at confidence 0.95.

gi_source.title: International tables of glycemic index and glycemic load values: 2021 — Atkinson et al.; analogous estimate based on mixed dish with peanut sauce and marinated grilled chicken
gi_source.organization: University of Sydney GI Database / American Journal of Clinical Nutrition
gi_source.source_match_notes: No direct GI measurement exists for chicken satay with peanut sauce in the University of Sydney GI Database. GI estimated at 50 (low) based on the analogous mixed dish profile: the peanut sauce contributes moderate sugar (14 g/100 g) and fat which attenuates glycemic response; dietary fibre at 2.83 g/100 g further moderates postprandial glucose. Glycemic load per 150 g serving = (50 × 15.36 × 1.5) / 100 = 11.5 g. This is a defensible analogous estimate per University of Sydney GI Database methodology for mixed dishes.

hypertension.key_factor: Sodium at 430 mg/100 g exceeds the 400 mg/100 g threshold; a 150 g serving delivers approximately 645 mg sodium, representing 28% of the 2300 mg daily limit.
hypertension.guidance: Individuals with hypertension should limit portion size to 2–3 skewers, request reduced-salt marinade, use peanut sauce sparingly, and avoid pairing with other high-sodium hawker dishes in the same meal.

type2_diabetes.key_factor: Sugar at 14.0 g/100 g is close to the 15 g/100 g threshold; total carbohydrate at 15.36 g/100 g with a low estimated GI of 50 produces a moderate glycemic load of 11.5 per serving.
type2_diabetes.guidance: Individuals with Type 2 Diabetes should request peanut sauce on the side and use a small amount only, avoid pairing with ketupat (rice cake) to prevent compounding carbohydrate load, and monitor portion to 3–4 skewers to keep glycemic load within a single-meal target.

cardiovascular_disease.key_factor: Saturated fat from peanut sauce and coconut milk (estimated 2.1 g/100 g) combined with sodium at 430 mg/100 g presents a moderate cardiovascular risk profile; fat at 9.55 g/100 g is predominantly unsaturated from peanuts.
cardiovascular_disease.guidance: Individuals with cardiovascular disease may consume chicken satay in moderate portions (3–4 skewers) with minimal peanut sauce, favouring the lean grilled chicken component which provides high-quality protein (16.45 g/100 g) with relatively low saturated fat.

rheumatoid_arthritis.key_factor: Sodium at 430 mg/100 g exceeds the RA threshold for tissue swelling; sugar at 14.0 g/100 g approaches the 15 g/100 g pro-inflammatory threshold; peanut sauce with coconut milk adds saturated fat that may promote inflammatory cytokine activity.
rheumatoid_arthritis.guidance: Individuals with rheumatoid arthritis should limit peanut sauce to reduce saturated fat and sugar, request reduced sugar in peanut sauce preparation, and retain turmeric in the marinade as curcumin provides anti-inflammatory benefit.

llm_training.natural_language_description: Chicken satay with peanut sauce is a popular Singaporean and Malay hawker dish consisting of skinless, boneless chicken pieces marinated in a spiced blend of lemongrass, turmeric, galangal, coriander, cumin, sugar, and salt, then skewered on bamboo sticks and grilled or barbecued over charcoal until lightly charred and fragrant. The dish is served with a rich, sweet-savoury peanut sauce made from ground roasted peanuts, coconut milk, chilli, lemongrass, and palm sugar, alongside compressed rice cakes (ketupat) and fresh cucumber and onion slices. The chicken is tender and smoky with a slightly caramelised exterior, while the peanut sauce is thick, nutty, mildly spicy, and sweet. Nutritionally, per 100 g the dish provides 213 kcal, 16.45 g protein, 9.55 g fat, 15.36 g carbohydrate, 14.0 g sugar, 2.83 g dietary fibre, and 430 mg sodium. The high sugar content (14 g/100 g) is primarily from the peanut sauce and marinade, making portion control important for individuals managing diabetes or hypertension. The sodium level at 430 mg/100 g exceeds the 400 mg/100 g threshold relevant for hypertension management. The dish is halal-certified in most hawker contexts and is a high-protein option suitable for general consumption in moderate portions. It is commonly consumed as a snack, starter, or light dinner at Singaporean hawker centres, food courts, and Malay restaurants.
common_user_queries: How many calories in chicken satay with peanut sauce?

How many skewers is a serving of satay?

confidence_reason: Core nutrient values sourced directly from the HPB Singapore Foodo database (F11020021) at confidence 0.95. This is an exact match to the canonical Singapore food composition entry for Chicken Satay with Sauce. Saturated fat estimated at 2.1 g/100 g from USDA FoodData Central analogous entry for chicken satay (SR Legacy) as HPB does not report saturated fat breakdown; this is documented in notes. GI is an analogous estimate as no direct measurement exists.
notes: 1. Saturated fat (2.1 g/100 g) is estimated from USDA FoodData Central SR Legacy entry for chicken satay (closest approved-source analogue) as the HPB database does not report saturated fat breakdown for this entry. 2. monounsaturated_fat_g, polyunsaturated_fat_g, trans_fat_g, potassium_mg, calcium_mg, iron_mg, cholesterol_mg, vitamin_c_mg, vitamin_a_mcg_rae, vitamin_b1_thiamine_mg, vitamin_b2_riboflavin_mg, vitamin_b3_niacin_mg, vitamin_b6_mg, vitamin_b12_mcg, folate_mcg_dfe, vitamin_d_mcg, vitamin_e_mg, phosphorus_mg, magnesium_mg, zinc_mg, selenium_mcg, and water_g are set to -1: detailed micronutrient and fatty acid fractionation data for this traditional Singaporean preparation are not available in the HPB Singapore Foodo database or USDA FoodData Central as of 2026. 3. GI value of 50 is an analogous estimate; no direct GI measurement for chicken satay with peanut sauce is available in the University of Sydney GI Database as of 2026. 4. The input record listed 'plant_based' as a dietary tag, which is incorrect for a chicken-based dish; this tag has been removed. 5. The input GI value of 0 with confidence 0.438 from 'Embedded GI Database' has been replaced with a defensible analogous estimate from the University of Sydney GI Database methodology, as the original source is not an approved GI source. 6. FoodEx2 match_type corrected from 'analogous_estimate' to 'closest_parent' to conform to the permitted enumeration values ['closest_parent', 'exact', 'none'].

Iteration 2: Corrected gi_category to 'low' to align with GI 50; ensured aliases_by_language consistency with flat aliases array.

Iteration 3: Corrected ontology.foodex2_match_type from 'analogous_estimate' to 'closest_parent' to conform to permitted enumeration ['closest_parent', 'exact', 'none'], resolving G5 hard gate failure.
